coredata.dc:title: Cathodic nanoscale carbon chemically captures carbon Dioxide: Computational evidence through hybrid density functional theory
coredata.dc:creator: Chaban, Vitaly V.
Andreeva, Nadezhda A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dioxide (CO2) represents an industry-generated greenhouse gas, whose excessive presence in the atmosphere of the Earth is undesirable. Novel CO2 scavengers are being introduced nowadays to fix and valorize CO2 at the moment of its production. We herein invented an original solution for the CO2 chemisorption by the carbonaceous cathode at which voltage is applied. The electron-rich carbon atoms readily react with CO2 and produce carboxylated derivatives, which only remain stable as long as the external voltage remains applied. The kinetics and thermochemistry of the process are strongly sensitive to the electronic charge provided to the electrochemical setup. Upon increasing the carbonaceous cathode charge density from 21µCcm−2 to 127µCcm−2, the energy effect of the reaction decreases from 92 to −196 kJ mol−1. In turn, the height of the activation barrier decreases from 98 to 7 kJ mol−1. The voltage turnoff results in an activation barrier-free desorption of all the captured gas. The formation and ruination of the carbon(cathode)-carbon(CO2) covalent bond occur thanks to an additional electron. Otherwise, the reaction is thermochemically prohibited. By using a graphene quantum dot (GQD) as an idealized model of the nanoscale porous cathode, we report extensive characterization through geometrical, electronic, and energetic properties of the involved chemical transformations. Since the local atomic structures of GQD and various activated carbon species possessing nanopores are similar, the available cheaper carbonaceous materials should exhibit qualitatively the same scavenger performance. We argue that the invented method is technologically simple to implement and scale up.

Vibrational frequencies in reactants and products

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The reaction energy profile corresponds to CO2 reacting with the pristine GQD. The function verifies that no stable products are possible. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance.

The reaction coordinate for the CO2 reacting with the singly-negatively-charged GQD in water. The excess charge per carbon atom of the cathode amounts to e/46. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance.

The optimized molecular geometry of the CO2 molecule at the surface of the singly-negatively charged GQD. We designate such types of molecular configurations as the states of physisorption. The states of physisorption are separated from the states of chemisorption (carboxylated GQD) by the chemisorption activation barriers.
The molecular geometries of the singly-negatively charged GQD upon CO2 capture: (a) chemisorbed state and (b) transition state.

The evolution of the Merz-Kollman charge of (a) the region where the chemisorption reaction takes place and (b) the sorbate CO2 molecule for the singly-negatively-charged system. The depicted charges of the cathode were computed by summing up the partial atomic charges of the reaction site and carbon atoms linked to the reaction site. The depicted charges on the CO2 molecules were computed by summing up the charges on all atoms belonging to the CO2/carboxyl moiety.

The reaction energetic profile for CO2 reacting with the doubly-negatively-charged GQD in water. The excess charge per carbon atom of the cathode amounts to e/23. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance.

The reaction energetic profile for CO2 reacting with the triply-negatively-charged GQD in water. The excess charge per carbon atom of the cathode amounts to 3e/46. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance.

The reaction energetic coordinate for CO2 reacting with the quadruply-negatively-charged GQD in water. The excess charge per carbon atom of the cathode amounts to 2e/23. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance. The abrupt drop of potential energy at 0.26 nm manifests a spontaneous transformation of CO2 0 into CO2 –.

The reaction profile for CO2 reacting with the quintuply-negatively-charged GQD in water. The excess charge per carbon atom of the cathode amounts to 5e/46. The selected reaction coordinate is a carbon(GQD)–carbon(CO2) distance. The abrupt drop of potential energy at 0.26 nm manifests a spontaneous transformation of CO2 0 into CO2 –.

The energetic descriptors of the carboxylation reaction as a function of the cathodic charge. (Left) Energetic effect of the reaction. (Right) The height of the activation barrier. Upon placing the charge of minus six on the cathode, the chemisorption occurs without an activation barrier.

The distributions of partial electrostatic atomic charges over the carbon atoms in the (a) singly-negatively-charged GQD and (b) doubly-negatively-charged GQD. The charges of the rim hydrogen atoms were added to the charges of the carbon atoms, to which hydrogens are respectively linked.

The distributions of partial electrostatic atomic charges over the carbon atoms in the (a) triply-negatively-charged GQD and (b) quadruply-negatively-charged GQD. The charges of the rim hydrogen atoms were added to the charges of the carbon atoms, to which hydrogens are respectively linked.
The localizations of the HOMO and LUMO correspond to the optimized structure of the singly-negatively-charged GQD.
The localizations of the HOMOs and LUMOs correspond to the reaction product in the case of the doubly-negatively-charged GQD.
The infrared spectra corresponding to the products of physisorption for various applied charges of the cathode: (a) minus 2; (b) minus 3; (c) minus 4. Note that Vs stands for symmetric stretching.

The infrared spectra corresponding to the products of chemisorption for various applied charges of the cathode: (a) minus 2, (b) minus 3, (c) minus 4. Note that Vs is a symmetric stretching; Vas is an antisymmetric stretching.

The global reactivity parameters, such as ionization potential, electron affinity, band gap, electronegativity (χ), electronic chemical potential (μ), and electrophilicity index (ω), were computed for a series of the charged cathode species. The geometries of the physically adsorbed CO2 molecules in the differently charged systems were used.

The global reactivity parameters, such as ionization potential, electron affinity, band gap, electronegativity (χ), electronic chemical potential (μ), and electrophilicity index (ω), were computed for the chemisorption product as a function of the charge of the cathode species.
The Gibbs free energies correspond to the chemisorption reactions at a set of temperatures and ambient pressure for different charges of the cathode (GQD).
The enthalpies correspond to the chemisorption reactions at a set of temperatures and ambient pressure for different charges of GQD.
The isobaric heat capacities, standard entropies, and entropic factors, -TΔS, of the studied CO2 chemisorption reactions.
Macroscopic parameters characterizing the performance of the proposed CO2 scavenger.

head.ce:title: Cathodic nanoscale carbon chemically captures carbon Dioxide: Computational evidence through hybrid density functional theory

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dioxide (CO2) represents an industry-generated greenhouse gas, whose excessive presence in the atmosphere of the Earth is undesirable. Novel CO2 scavengers are being introduced nowadays to fix and valorize CO2 at the moment of its production. We herein invented an original solution for the CO2 chemisorption by the carbonaceous cathode at which voltage is applied. The electron-rich carbon atoms readily react with CO2 and produce carboxylated derivatives, which only remain stable as long as the external voltage remains applied. The kinetics and thermochemistry of the process are strongly sensitive to the electronic charge provided to the electrochemical setup. Upon increasing the carbonaceous cathode charge density from 21µCcm−2 to 127µCcm−2, the energy effect of the reaction decreases from 92 to −196 kJ mol−1. In turn, the height of the activation barrier decreases from 98 to 7 kJ mol−1. The voltage turnoff results in an activation barrier-free desorption of all the captured gas. The formation and ruination of the carbon(cathode)-carbon(CO2) covalent bond occur thanks to an additional electron. Otherwise, the reaction is thermochemically prohibited. By using a graphene quantum dot (GQD) as an idealized model of the nanoscale porous cathode, we report extensive characterization through geometrical, electronic, and energetic properties of the involved chemical transformations. Since the local atomic structures of GQD and various activated carbon species possessing nanopores are similar, the available cheaper carbonaceous materials should exhibit qualitatively the same scavenger performance. We argue that the invented method is technologically simple to implement and scale up.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Global warming is an acute ecological problem that causes numerous undoubtedly negative effects on the planet [1]. First, agricultural yields decrease, as some plants are unable to grow so well at warmer temperatures. Food security for billions of people around the globe is, therefore, threatened. Second, more extreme weather events, such as heat waves, droughts, floods, and wildfires, are routinely discussed in the context of global warming. Third, the sea levels rise since the glaciers and ice caps are melting. The coastal communities around the world, as well as low-lying islands, appear at a fairly realistic risk. Fourth, many species poorly adapt to the changing climate. This trend results in the loss of biodiversity and exhibits a devastating impact on ecosystems globally. Finally, global warming is causing health problems, such as heat stress, respiratory problems, and vector-borne diseases. These problems are especially acute in developing countries, where people have fewer resources to adapt [2].

Extreme weather events damage infrastructure, such as roads, bridges, and power lines. Consequently, this leads to a disruption of transportation, communication, and essential services. Global warming is already costing the world economy billions of dollars each year. These costs are expected to keep increasing in the future. As an unfavorable climate evolution makes some areas of the world less habitable, people are forced to migrate to other areas. This could eventually lead to serious social unrest and conflict.

The negative effects of global warming are already being felt everywhere, and they are only going to get worse if we do not take action. It is important to act now to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and mitigate the effects of climate change [1]. The most abundant greenhouse gas whose concentration is associated with human technologies, is carbon dioxide (CO2). It emits due to burning fossil fuels [3]. The development of CO2 scavengers has been an active area of research for many years [4–10]. Several chemical technologies have been proposed and urge feasibility increases, each with pros and cons [11–14].

Chemical absorbers are materials that react covalently with CO2 to form a solid or liquid product. For instance, aqueous calcium hydroxide reacts with CO2 to form calcium carbonate, which is a solid [15]. Such a method is referred to as carbonate looping [16]. In turn, physical absorbers represent materials that physically adsorb CO2 onto their surfaces. Recall that activated carbon is a good adsorbent for many polar and non-polar gases including CO2 [17]. Biological absorbers are deemed to be a very promising direction of development with yet poorly understood potential and scalability. These are microorganisms that can consume CO2 as food using carbon atoms as construction bricks for their organisms. Certain existing bacteria can be used to remove CO2 from the air.

Autotrophic bacteria find CO2 digestible and nutritious enough to use it as a sole source of their carbon [18]. Thus, they elaborated mechanisms to convert CO2 into biomass. Cyanobacteria, microalgae, and certain types of archaea follow the mentioned route. Cyanobacteria employ photosynthesis to convert CO2 into biomass. Heterotrophic bacteria use CO2 as an additional source of carbon as soon as they do not have more energetic organic matter to consume. Methylotrophs retrieve carbon out of methane and methanol, whereas CO2 can be supplementary food. Methylobacterium converts CO2 into methanol. There are specific species of bacteria that valorize CO2 by producing common reactants, such as methane, ethylene, acetone, and isopropanol [18–19]. We hereby stress the CO2 chemisorption and CO2 valorization reactions invented by bacteria because live organisms provide researchers with an invaluable source of hints of how greener technologies must perform. The world of CO2 chemisorption appears to be miraculously versatile, and magnificent solutions are just around, in nature.

Electrochemical CO2 capture designates a group of processes that use electricity to selectively remove CO2 from a gas mixture [20–21]. Electrochemical reactions have the potential to be more efficient and cost-effective than more traditional methods of CO2 capture [22–23]. A common approach is to use a reversible redox reaction to capture CO2. A CO2-sensitive molecule is electrochemically reduced, forming a species that can bind to CO2. The CO2-bound species are then oxidized, releasing CO2 and regenerating the CO2-sensitive molecule. Another approach is to use a pH swing. The pH of a solution is cycled between acidic and basic conditions. Since CO2 is more soluble in acidic solutions, the pH must be cycled to an acidic state to absorb the gas into the solution. To release CO2, the swing of pH to the basic condition is performed [24].

We expect electrochemical CO2 capture to be more efficient since the energy required to capture CO2 is provided via electricity, rather than heat. Furthermore, electrochemical CO2 capture can be more cost-effective, as the required electricity can be generated from renewable sources. Finally, the electrochemical route is seen as more flexible and can be used with a variety of gas mixtures [25–26]. A possible challenge is that the considered electrochemical reactions involved in CO2 capture are slow. The CO2-sensitive molecules are expensive to synthesize. The development of more efficient and selective electrochemical reactions together with lowering the cost of CO2-sensitive molecules represent urgent tasks.

As we exemplified above, the development of various CO2 scavengers represents a vigorous and versatile area of research [27–30]. Several challenges must be addressed before these technologies can be deployed. NovelCO2 scavengers need to be cost-effective to be widely adopted. Novel CO2 scavengers must be scalable to remove CO2 from large volumes. Novel CO2 scavengers must be durable to operate for long periods without a need for replacement. Finally, novel CO2 scavengers must have a low environmental impact to be sustainable [31–34].

In the present work, we report a direct carboxylation of the sp2-carbon species by CO2 upon applying voltage. The voltage is the most robust means to deposit additional electrons into the chemical structure of the semiconducting CO2 scavenger. The accepted additional electrons transform otherwise inert sp2-hybridized carbon atoms into avid reaction centers hosting straightforward carboxylation by CO2. Since the capacity of the solid CO2 scavenger depends on the available surface area, nanoporous carbon shall be considered preferable [35–38]. A graphene quantum dot (GQD) was used as a computationally convenient model for the nanoscale carbon simulations. By considering several combinations of chemisorption parameters, we identified the most applicable one and provided an in-depth justification of the newly proposed reaction pathway at atomistic and electronic levels of resolution.

The GQD of composition C46H18 was used to mimic the carbonaceous sorbent. The hydrogen atoms were used to saturate the dangling bonds of the carbon atoms. For certain trial calculations, a smaller GQD of the composition of C16H10 was used. If an obtained result deserved subsequent exploration, the simulations were repeated with the larger molecule. The initial location of the CO2 molecule was obtained via the minimization of all forces in a simulated system. All reported calculations used the same level of theory and all convergence criteria as documented below.

We recorded reaction profiles for the CO2 chemisorption by GQD by computing a fraction of the potential energy surface in the framework of hybrid density functional theory (HDFT). The molecular wave functions, equilibrium electronic structures, charge density distributions, and molecular geometries of reactants, products, and transition states were obtained using the range-separated exchange–correlation meta-GGA functional M11 [39]. The molecular wave functions were constructed out of the primitive atomic orbitals according to the atom-centered double-zeta split-valence polarized 6–31 + G(d) basis set. The modern HDFT that we opted to employ implicitly includes empirical correction parameters to accurately reproduce the applicable weak long-range attractive (London) forces [40].

The wave function convergence criterion within the self-consistent field procedure was set to 10–7 atomic energy units. The rational function optimization algorithm was used to optimize fully restrained and partially restrained geometries along the hypothesized minimum reaction path. The following geometry convergence criteria were chosen: 120 kJ nm−1 for the largest force, 80 kJ nm−1 for root-mean-squared force, 1.8 × 10–3 nm for the largest displacement between subsequent steps, and 1.2 × 10-3 nm for root-mean-squared displacement. The convergence of the force-minimization procedure assumed that all four thresholds were fulfilled simultaneously.

The simulated reactions were explored by forcibly moving the chosen reaction coordinate along the desired direction with a uniform linear step of 0.005 nm. As such, partial geometry optimizations were carried out so that all unrestrained internal coordinates adapt to a single reaction coordinate that evolves. The total potential energies of a series of configurations were plotted against the reaction coordinate. Note that the initial and final states of the simulations represent local minimum stationary points. Saddle points, in turn, represent energy maxima located between the initial and final states. The potential energy difference between the initial state and the saddle point represents the height of the activation barrier. The potential energy difference between the initial state and the deepest minimum state, if any, represents the energetic effect of the reaction. The valid identification of the local maximum was confirmed by determining a single imaginary frequency in the vibrational profile. Note that the potential energy surface scan calculations represent a highly expensive and time-consuming sort of research. The hereby reported results are based on several thousand single-point self-consistent field calculations consequently conducted. All wave functions related to the reaction profiles were solved via unrestricted DFT calculations.

The simulations included the effects of hydration. Water was represented as an implicit medium according to the polarizable continuum model. The implicit solvation model supplements the Hamiltonian of the simulated system with a mathematical term that describes solvent–solute coupling based on the local electric field created by the solute, i.e., the simulated system. The hydration was applied along the entire reaction path because the reactants, transition state complex, and product are expected to exhibit different levels of hydrophilicity. The effect of implicit hydration was also added to the thermochemical calculations.

The analysis of harmonic frequencies was carried out using the rigid rotor approximation. The frequency profiles contained no imaginary frequencies for local minimum states and a single imaginary frequency per profile for the transition states. The results were used to compute near- and far-infrared spectra and obtain the zero-point energy corrections for the reported thermochemical potentials.

The chosen molecular configurations were described by their geometrical parameters (covalent and non-covalent distances and angles) and partial electrostatic charges. The latter were obtained according to the Merz-Singh-Kollman algorithm [41]. We use partial atomic charges as a direct measure of the nucleophilicity and electrophilicity of the interaction centers that determine the thermochemistry and activation barriers of the studied reactions.

Gaussian’09D software [42] was called iteratively to conduct electronic-structure calculations. Gaussian’09D was available in the high-performance supercomputing research center TORNADO at the affiliation of the second author. The calculations were executed in parallel by using 56 threads per every instance of a quantum-chemical calculation. Visual Molecular Dynamics software (ver.1.9.3) [43] and Gabedit software (ver.2.5.2) [44] were used to create, edit, and propagate the Z–matrices, and prepare molecular graphics. The in-house programming developments were used to implement non-standard procedures and automate research stages.

Pristine carbonaceous structures, such as activated carbon, graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes, and numerous other particles, in which carbon atoms are preferentially arranged as cycles with sp2-hybridization, cannot react with CO2 directly, i.e., without being deliberately functionalized [45–46]. For instance, the amino groups can be grafted to the surface of graphene so that the latter could work as aqueous amine CO2 scavengers [47]. Throughout this work, we use a model of a graphene quantum dot (GQD) to discuss carbonaceous cathodes. GQD is by definition a small sheet of GQD whose band gap depends on the size. The model employed herein contains 46 carbons atoms organized as fused six-membered rings. The structure mimics the surfaces of graphene and graphite. The edge carbon atoms of GQD are saturated with a suitable number of hydrogen atoms for the carbonaceous molecule to preserve its expected electronic structure and physicochemical behavior. The employed model is chemically justified to consider reactions of graphite, graphene, activated carbon species, and similar atomistic ensembles. The simulated CO2 chemisorption reactions were hosted by the central carbon atom of GQD, which appeared closest to the CO2 molecule after the geometry optimization of the non-covalently bound sorbent-sorbate complex.

Although the fused carbon rings possess aromatic delocalized electrons, they prefer not to participate in potential bonding with CO2. The most intuitive reason for such a phenomenon is that, upon the reaction of carboxylation, CO2 transforms into the carboxyl group. The carboxyl group must be negatively charged to remain thermodynamically stable. At the discussed point of the prospective reaction, the system does not possess an extra electron as it already used one electron to create the carbon–carbon covalent bond uniting GQD and CO2. The provision of the required extra electron is expected to eliminate the problem and make the reaction proceed further. There are analogies of the described mechanism with the carboxamidation reaction in aqueous amines and the carboxylation reaction in tetralkylphosphonium/trimethylsulfonium ylides [48–51].

We start with the blunt carboxylation of pristine GQD. The pristine GQD can be seen as a non-charged cathode. While this reaction is expected to fail, its energetic profile provides one with useful information on how much the process is thermochemically prohibited. Fig. 1 confirms the impossibility of a direct reaction between CO2 and GQD. Since there is no strong attraction between the carbon atom of CO2 and the carbon atom of GQD, the reaction can barely start. Since there is not enough excessive electron density within the conduction band of GQD, the possible carboxyl group, as depicted, cannot stabilize. The energy profile recorded along the tested reaction minimum path, therefore, does not contain either a transition state or a minimum corresponding to the CO2 chemisorption product. The plotted potential energies versus distance may be useful for point-by-point comparison with the below-considered case of increased carbon nucleophilicities. At the reaction coordinate, roughly 0.16 nm, which would otherwise correspond to the formation of the carboxylated product, the potential energy penalty amounts to + 300 kJ/mol. To illustrate an obvious idea, we deliberately used a smaller GQD, composed of the four fused rings, to save computational resources.

The reaction energy profile provided in Fig. 1 is also useful in the context of CO2 desorption as soon as an external voltage turns off. The charge of the cathode becomes zero, consequently, the system energy decays exactly as shown from left to right. The energy gain of about 300 kJ/mol corresponds to the removal of each CO2 molecule. The full discharge is assumed unless the carboxyl groups protonate for some reason upon gas absorption. If protonation takes place, the system acquires a metastable state, graphene-(COOH)n, and the due discharge of the electrode may get complicated. The electrons of the carboxyl groups are lower in potential energy as compared to those belonging to the π-subsystem of graphene. The electrically supplied energy would effectively be consumed and trapped in the carboxylation reaction. To avoid this certainly undesirable reaction pathway leading to the aging of the cathode, the medium of the reaction must not be acidic whatsoever.

The role of a polar solvent, such as water or ethanol, is seen as positive for CO2 capture since polar liquid molecules interact more strongly with the carboxyl group (in the state products) than with CO2 (in the state of reactants). Therefore, the chemisorption reaction stabilizes enthalpically, for instance, due to the hydrogen bond formation between a solvent and a carboxyl group. However, such a promoting polar solvent must have low acidity to prevent the protonation of the carboxyl moieties. The existence of multiple protonated carboxyl groups on the surface of the cathode would also somewhat stabilize the state of the product thanks to electrostatic attractions between them, whereas deprotonated carboxyl groups electrostatically repel and can be easily detached upon discharge/voltage removal.

The electrodes made of carbon are widely employed in high-performance energy storage rechargeable devices thanks to a set of favorable electronic properties and chemical stabilities. Being a part of the cathode in a certain electric setup, the carbonaceous structure obtains an excessive electron density, which is distributed within its conduction band. The separation of charges is intended to create a difference of potentials and, thus, store the electrostatic energy. However, it also changes the physical and chemical properties of the cathode. The adjusted reactivity of the electron-rich electrode can be deliberately used to conduct desirable chemical transformations.

Fig. 2 depicts the reaction profiles in water for CO2 capture by a singly-negatively-charged GQD. The energetic effect of the CO2 chemisorption by the cathodic GQD in water amounts to + 92 kJ/mol. The activation barrier is 98 kJ/mol. The saddle points locate at the reaction coordinates of 0.21–0.22 nm, which are somewhat more than 30% larger than the coordinate of the minimum state. In turn, the coordinates of the minimum corresponding to the carboxylated product appear close to 0.16 nm. The mentioned distance is within sound chemical expectations for the carboxyl group emerging on the surface of GQD. Since the valence band of GQD gets additional electron density, e/46 per carbon atom, the nucleophilicities of all carbon atoms belonging to GQD increase. As a result, it becomes possible to conduct the reaction somehow analogous to carboxamidation with fairly competitive thermodynamic parameters [47]. We previously described reminiscent CO2 chemisorption reactions by phosphonium, sulfonium, and hypothetic ammonium ylides, in which cases the carboxylated carbon atom also exhibits the deliberately increased nucleophilicity.

The potential energy plateau between the reaction coordinates of 0.165 and 0.201 nm reflects a relatively low capability of the GQD to capture CO2 at a small charging degree. Yet, the minimum corresponding to the product of chemisorption exists. Noteworthy, the covalent angle in the CO2 molecule decreases from 142 down to 129 degrees while the reactants move leftward onto the energy plateau.

Fig. 3 depicts the energy-minimized non-reacted configuration of CO2 and the singly-charged cathode. The sorbate molecule locates in the parallel plane relative to GQD, whereas the closest distance between them amounts to 0.320 nm. The detected distance well agrees with the sum of the van der Waals radii of two carbon atoms. The attraction takes place thanks to the London forces and induced partial atomic charges on the charged cathode made of GQD. The carbon atom of CO2 is positively charged, +0.829e, because it is covalently bonded to the two oxygen atoms.

The molecular configurations corresponding to the state of the physisorbed CO2 molecule have been identified in systems with charges −1e through −5e. On the contrary, in the system with the −6e charge, no physisorption is possible because CO2 readily transforms into the carboxyl moiety and joins GQD. No activation barrier separates chemisorption and physisorption in the mentioned system with the highest trialed negative charge. It is noteworthy that the charge of the cathode fairly weakly impacts the optimal sorbent-sorbate distance upon physical sorption. Compare 0.32 nm for the GQD charge of minus two electrons, 0.31 nm for the charge of minus three electrons, and 0.29 nm for the charge of minus four electrons. As the integral charge of the cathode grows, the Coulombic energy contribution to the sorbent-sorbate binding energy increases.

The energetic difference between the state of the reactants and the energy of the transition state (Fig. 4 ) constitutes the height of the activation barrier. The geometry of the transition state molecular complex is expected to be between the geometries of the reactants and products. However, the transition geometry should not be equally distant from the two above-mentioned geometries. We found the transition state geometry to be located at the reaction coordinate of 0.195 nm. In turn, the product state corresponds to the reaction coordinate of 0.168 nm. The carbon(GQD)-carbon(CO2) covalent bond appears substantially weaker than the conventional carbon–carbon single covalent bond, which is 0.154 nm long. The elongation of the bond happens due to the geometrical restrictions at the surface of GQD. In particular, the GQD-CO2 bond must be perpendicular to the plane of the sorbent. The CO2 molecule adjusts its valence angle, O-C-O, from 180 degrees in the gaseous sorbate to 130 degrees in the chemisorbed sorbate. The bending of CO2 takes place before the molecule reaches the transition state. For instance, in the transition state, CO2 visually resembles the carboxyl moiety to way larger extent than the gaseous molecule. The alteration of the sorbate geometry occurs due to the change of the hybridization state and the acquisition of the extra valence electron. The bond multiplicities within the O-C-O entity evolve from two in the gaseous state to one and a half in the carboxylated state.

We studied all the reactant, transition state, and product molecular geometries for the integral cathode charges from −1e to −6e. The increase in the electronic charge decreases the length of the GQD-carboxyl covalent bond, i.e., makes this specific bond more thermodynamically favorable. In the meantime, the covalent angle of CO2 in the transition state does not exhibit a uniform trend versus the number of available excess electrons. This observation means that the carboxylation reaction depends on numerous factors simultaneously. While the aggregate charge of the cathode is the major one, certain secondary parameters depend on the local electron density distribution over the benzene ring that hosts the carboxylation reaction.

The GQD-carboxyl bond lengths amount to 0.162, 0.161, 0.160, 0.160, and 0.160 nm for the systems bearing the charges of –2e, –3e, –4e, –5e, and –6e. It is noteworthy that the difference between the cathodes possessing the –1e and –2e charges is much higher as compared to the length decreases due to the further charge increases. The GQD-carboxyl lengths in the transition states of the above-enumerated systems are 0.204 (–3e), 0.223 (–4e), 0.259 (–5e), and 0.261 (–6e)nm. One, therefore, concludes that the stepwise increase in the nucleophilicity of GQD shifts the carbon–carbon distances in the transition states to substantially larger values. The carboxyl moiety covalent angles, O-C-O, amount to 127, 126, 125, 124, and 124 degrees for the systems bearing the charges of –2e, –3e, –4e, –5e, and –6e. That is, a higher cathode charge makes the covalent angle slightly less obtuse. In the transition states, the discussed angles are 142, 135, 164, and 167 degrees for the charges of –2e, –3e, –4e, and –5e. Recall that no transition state exists in the system with the –6e aggregate charge.

The approach of CO2 to the reaction site at the GQD results in an increase of the electronic density both on the sorbent and sorbate moieties (Fig. 5 ). At the reaction coordinate of 0.32 nm, the total electrostatic charge of the reaction region is –0.18e. However, as the reaction coordinate decreases down to 0.21 nm, the charge decreases up to –1.0e and remains so upon further approach of the reactants to one another. Interestingly, the total electrostatic charge of the CO2 molecule does not change until the prospective reacting atoms come as close as 0.22 nm. The charge decreases from –0.19e to –1.14e as the distance decreases from 0.22 to 0.15 nm. The charge of –1e is an essential prerequisite for the thermodynamic stabilization of the chemisorption product as the carboxyl group must possess a singlet electronic configuration. The electrostatic repulsion between the reaction site of GQD and CO2 is responsible for the activation reaction barrier. The accumulation of electrons at the reaction site is necessary to create the GQD-CO2 covalent bond and transform the CO2 molecule into the negatively charged carboxyl moiety.

Similar trends have also been detected for the other charges of the cathode. The maximum electron excess localized over the reaction regions amounts to –1.2e, –1.5e, –1.6e, and –1.8e for the aggregate charges of the cathode amounting to –2e, –3e, –4e, and –5e. Therefore, the nucleophilicity of the sorbent increases quite uniformly upon the cathode charging. A higher nucleophilicity of the reaction center at the cathode appears in perfect agreement with lower activation barriers and more favorable thermochemistry of the carboxylation reaction. Another important observation is that CO2 becomes –COO– upon the reaction coordinate evolution between the transition state and the product minimum state. A higher cathode charge fosters anionization of CO2, which starts at somewhat larger reaction coordinates.

Figs. 6-7
provide reaction profiles for the cathode charges of –2e and –3e. In the former case, the transition state was found at the reaction coordinate of 0.204 nm, and the product state was found at the reaction coordinate of 0.162 nm. In the latter case, the transition state was found at the reaction coordinate of 0.215 nm, and the product state was found at the reaction coordinate of 0.161 nm. That is, the increase in the applied voltage modestly impacts the values of the reaction coordinates at the critical points. In the meantime, the height of the activation barrier decreases by 18 kJ/mol between the cathode charges of −2e and –3e. Similarly, the thermochemical effect decreases even more impressively, by 49 kJ/mol. These results motivated us to continue trialing higher voltages, i.e., higher electrode charges.

The covalent O-C-O angle only slightly decreases between the transition state and the product state. For instance, in the triply-negatively-charged system, this angle changes from 135 to 126 degrees. The evolution of the covalent O-C-O angle is highly important because it is a primary geometrical descriptor of the studied carboxylation reaction along with the length of the emerging QGD-CO2 covalent bond.

Figs. 8-9
depict the reaction energetic profiles for the high degrees of the cathode charging, 2e/23 and 5e/46, or quadruply- and quintuply-charged GQD. In these systems, the activation barriers virtually disappear becoming 12 and 7 kJ/mol for the −4e and –5e charging states, respectively. Such barriers can be easily overcome upon thermal motion at standard conditions. The energy effects of the reactions amount to –177 and –196 kJ/mol, respectively. At the high cathode charges, the CO2 chemisorption is a strongly thermochemically favorable process. The chemisorption gets allowed because the system possesses enough electrons to build new covalent bonds and stabilize the emerging anionic species.

The GQD-CO2 covalent bond lengths equal 0.162 and 0.160 nm in the –4e and –5e systems. There are two transition states per energy profile in these systems. Some part of the significant amount of the electron density, which is initially given to GQD, spontaneously migrates to CO2 as soon as the distance between the reactants becomes 0.259 nm (for the charge of –4e) and 0.263 nm (for the charge of –5e). As a result of such an electron transfer, CO2 transforms into the carboxyl moiety, i.e., decreases the O-C-O covalent angle, without yet forming a covalent bond with the cathode. A significant drop of potential energy (Figs. 8-9) accompanies this electron transfer. The ionization of CO2 is associated with the small activation barriers of 12 and 7 kJ/mol relative to the states of unperturbed reactants.

The second transition state locates at 0.238 nm (for the charge of –4e) and 0.246 nm (for the charge of –5e) values of the reaction coordinate. This transition state corresponds to the covalent bonding of the carboxyl group with GQD. The activation barrier computed versus the closest local minimum state amounts to 17 (for the charge of –4e) and 10 kJ/mol (for the charge of –5e).

Fig. 10 summarizes the effect of the cathode charge on the energetics of the carboxylation (CO2 chemisorption) reaction. The heights of the activation barrier and the total thermal effect of the reaction decrease uniformly as the voltage increases, and therefore, the charge of the cathode gets more negative. The activation barrier height is negligible at the charges of −4e and more negative ones. In turn, the reaction appears exothermic at the charges of –3e and more negative ones. Therefore, it might be reasonable to consider the charge of −2e/23 per carbon atom to be the most efficient setup for CO2 chemisorption.

The increase in electron density within GQD before the CO2 chemisorption initiates is not uniform (Fig. 11 ). Furthermore, not all carbon atoms of the simulated GQD exhibit negative charges. The immediate partial charge depends on the location of the carbon atom and its atomistic environment. For instance, the most nucleophilic sites are rim carbon atoms with partial charges of –0.5e (upon the aggregate cathode charge of –1e) and –0.6e (upon the aggregate cathode charge of –2e). The central carbons have partial charges between –0.1e and –0.2e, while some of them remain nearly neutral. Some of the central carbons possess charges of –0.3e and –0.4e. In turn, their neighboring carbons are positively charged, +0.1e through + 0.2e. Water adds certain electronic polarization to the charged GQD species as compared to the simulation in vacuo (not depicted). It is also essential to note that partial charge distributions are highly sensitive to the reaction coordinate and change virtually at every reaction step. Nevertheless, such results are useful to at least qualitatively rate the nucleophilicities of carbon as a function of the cathode charge and the stage of the carboxylation reaction.

Upon the charge assignment procedure, which is the fitting of the ab initio-derived electrostatic potential to a set of atomic nuclear positions, the rim carbon atoms saturated with hydrogens were treated as united CH sites. The assigned charges to the CH moieties appear more negative than an average carbon atom for a given cathode charge (see atoms #29–45, Fig. 12 ). Note that the partial charges are strongly susceptible to the surrounding electric field and the approach of CO2 results in drastic changes over the cathode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localizations of electronic orbitals and global reactivity parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The highest occupies molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) were analyzed for the optimized configurations of the reactants and products (Figs. 13-14
). The optimized configuration of the reactants de facto corresponds to the state of physically adsorbed CO2 on the carbonaceous surface. Recall that the chemisorbed and physisorbed states of CO2 are separated by the energy barriers of various heights along the reaction coordinate in five out of the six simulated degrees of the cathode charge.

In the physisorbed state, both HOMO and LUMO localize on GQD and correspond to the pi-electrons of the carbon atoms. The depicted orbitals high-energy orbitals are distributed throughout the cathode surface. Note that HOMO and LUMO belong only to the core region of the GQD, whereas the hydrogen atoms possess their s-electrons with way smaller potential energies. It allows one to conclude that the role of the edges of GQD in the carboxylation reaction is limited. The HOMO of CO2 is lower in energy compared to the depicted valence orbitals of GQD. There are no intersections of the orbitals belonging to the carbonaceous cathode and CO2 before the chemisorption takes place. When CO2 changes the geometry and, therefore, its valence electronic structure, to those of the carboxyl group, the highest energy electron initially belonging to GQD migrates to the oxygen atoms of carboxyl. We unraveled this phenomenon while considering partial charge distributions over atoms upon the stepwise mutual approach of the reactants.

In turn, the HOMO and LUMO in the chemisorbed state do not localize over the entire cathode. Instead, they are partially shared by the GQD moiety and the carboxyl moiety (Fig. 14). In addition to the distance-based criterion of the covalent bond formation, the shared valence orbitals confirm CO2 chemisorption on the charged graphene surface. We noted that the localizations of HOMOs and LUMOs are qualitatively identical for the singly- can doubly-charged cathode species in both physisorbed and chemisorbed CO2 molecular configurations. Figures S1-S4 contain additional molecular orbitals characterizing physical and chemical CO2 sorption for an interested reader.

Tables 1-2
summarize certain global reactivity parameters to compare the reactivities of the differently charged cathodes as a function of their aggregate charges and the states of chemisorbed CO2. The ionization potentials decrease monotonically as well as the band gaps upon the cathode charge increase. The most important parameter is the electrophilicity index, which is inversely proportional to the nucleophilicity index. The electrophilicity of the cathode decreases upon charging and, thus, the nucleophilicity increases. The nucleophilicity of the carbon atoms of GQD determines their ability to react with the approaching electrophilic carbon atom of CO2. Interestingly, the order of electrophilicities breaks between the charging states of −4e and –5e. We believe that such behavior is connected to the differences between the open-shell and closest-shell electronic configurations in these systems. For instance, the open-shell configurations have different energy levels of HOMO than the closed-shell configurations. We also note that the trends in other descriptors also appear smoother if one performs comparisons among singlet and doublet electronic configurations separately.

The grafting of the carboxyl moiety to GQD drastically changes the global reactivity descriptors, as provided in Table 2. This happens because the structures of the valence electron levels alternate in the GQD both due to the addition of new energy levels and due to the newly emerged carbon–carbon covalent bond. Still, the nucleophilicity remains to be in direct proportion to the aggregate charge of the cathode. This observation means that the carboxylated GQD retains a potential to react with additional CO2 molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vibrational frequencies in reactants and products
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 15-16
provide near- and far-infrared spectra for physisorbed and chemisorbed CO2 for several degrees of the cathode charging. The increase of the absolute value of the charge of GQD leads to a wide range of frequencies of the symmetric stretching vibrations. The peak widening takes place from 648 to 659 cm−1 to 550–659 cm−1. The carbonyl group symmetric stretching in CO2, according to the experimental data,[52] manifests around 630 cm−1. For the cases of larger cathode charges, the frequencies of the C = O vibrations in CO2 decrease somewhat.

As a result of the CO2 chemisorption, a new peak emerges (Fig. 15). This peak represents a symmetric C(GQD)-C(CO2) vibration around 800 cm−1. The degree of charging slightly shifts this peak, however, there is no qualitative trend versus the cathode charge. The antisymmetric stretching shifts leftward as the charge of the cathode grows. The corresponding vibrational frequencies equal 1300 cm−1 for the charge of minus two and 1382 cm−1 for the charge of minus four.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermochemistry of chemisorption versus the temperature of the reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
Tables 3-5
summarize thermodynamic quantities characterizing the CO2 chemisorption. In the case of −1e-5e cathode charge, the chemisorption thermodynamic potentials are given versus the physisorption state. In turn, in the case of the –6e cathode charge, the potentials are given versus the ideal gas state. This is done because there is no physisorption state for so strongly charged cathode as there is no activation barrier that separates physisorption and chemisorption. As it has already been demonstrated for the case of internal electronic energy, the enthalpies, and Gibbs free energies substantially decrease as the charge of GQD increases. The CO2 chemisorption is enthalpically driven. The fraction of the Gibbs free energy gain obtained thanks to the enthalpy alteration is 4–6 times higher as compared to the entropic contribution. Furthermore, the entropic contribution is expectedly unfavorable since chemisorption decreases the conformational freedom of the simulated system.

The increase in temperature leads to a somewhat more favorable enthalpy of reaction alteration. This trend is characteristic of all charging degrees of the carbonaceous cathode. In the meantime, the Gibbs free energy increases as the temperature rises. This trend takes place because the entropic penalty contribution, -TΔS, which is unfavorable, uniformly increases as the temperature increases.

The absolute value of the chemisorption entropic effect significantly increases as the cathode obtains higher amounts of electron density. We interpret this observation as a stronger chemical fixation of CO2 in the presence of excess electrons in the system. Stronger fixation of the sorbate molecule decreases its conformational flexibility and, consequently, increases the entropic penalty relative to the freestanding molecular configuration of CO2 in the gaseous phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications of the obtained results
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregate charges of the cathode applied in the present work and partial charges of the carbon atoms belonging to the cathode correspond to the justified magnitudes of applied voltage (Table6 ). By considering the size of the cathode and the deposited excess charge, one evaluates the associated voltage and energy cost of the elementary process of CO2 chemisorption.

The surface charge density on the cathode amounts to:

where q is the electronic charge and A is the surface area. Consequently, the field strength (

For the sake of this calculation, we assume that one plate of the hypothetical scavenger is charged and the other plate is not. Next, assuming the separation of the electrodes to be d = 5 nm, we get macroscopic descriptors of the electrochemical CO2 scavenger provided in Table 6.

The above-reported values of voltage do not account for the dielectric constant of the medium. If we place an isolating material between the charged carbonaceous species, the electric field will decrease in direct proportion to the dielectric constant of the isolator. Such a trick can, for instance, allow one to increase the thickness of the interelectrode separation should it be technically required during real-world implementation.

During our simulations, the cathodes maintain structural stabilities, even at the highest degree of charging. This observation reflects the existence of a local minimum on the potential energy surface that corresponds to the integrity of the simulated structures. At the same time, we did not investigate the kinetic stabilities of the charged graphene cathodes and did not consider possible adverse reactions of such charged carbonaceous cathodes with the environment. The quantum-chemical simulations suggested that even a modest applied charge, such as e/46 per carbon atom, fosters the carboxylation reaction.

The reaction profile reveals a minimum state corresponding to the carboxylated graphene product. This is in sharp contrast with the non-charged graphene, in which case any carboxylation is thermochemically forbidden. The further increase in the charge of the cathode heavily decreases the activation barrier and makes the reaction energy effect favorable. In the case of the 2e/23 charge per carbon atom, the carboxylation proceeds nearly spontaneously. The removal of voltage leads to an immediate decarboxylation of the cathode because the potential energy surface changes, see Fig. 1.

While the elaborated method requires a due technical adjustment considering specific details of the application, the reported simulations unequivocally prove the fundamental viability of the carboxylation reactions on pristine graphene and link microscopic parameters to the macroscopic descriptors, such as voltage and involved energetic costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and possible pitfalls of the performed study
ce:section.ce:para: We used the harmonic rigid rotor approximation to compute the zero-point energy correction, which represents a relatively computationally inexpensive approximation. A more accurate but way more expensive workflow might have been followed to obtain an anharmonic zero-point energy correction by including torsional anharmonicities [53]. In the case of flexible molecular geometries and the reactions with small thermochemical effects, the inclusion of torsional anharmonicities into the procedure of calculating activation barriers and Gibbs free energies may essentially adjust the predicted energetics.

The reported simulated systems have a finite size. Therefore, they have inevitable boundary effects. The boundary effect influences band gap, band structure, and excited states. However, the impact of the boundary effect on the chemical reactions occurring at a specific interaction site is insignificant. To minimize the impact of the rim atoms, we simulated the CO2 chemisorption at the carbon atoms, which were farthest from the edges of the graphene sheet. We deliberately opted to use non-periodic wave functions in the present study to apply a higher level of theory, such as hybrid density functional theory, and retain more versatile methodological means to analyze the results.

This work does not account for the carboxylation of the rim carbon atoms since we were primarily interested in the behavior of the negatively-charged graphene sheet. We assume that the overall number of symmetrically identical carbon atoms in real-world carbonaceous cathode much exceeds the number of the edge carbon atoms and the carbon atoms involved in structural defects. It might be interesting, in the future, to consider how the proximity of the hydrogen atoms or other moieties, such as the ones abundant in graphene oxide, [54–55] influence the thermochemical effects of the investigated chemisorption reactions. The presence of the hetero atoms may substantially adjust the reaction barriers because the approaching CO2 molecules interact non-covalently with all groups surrounding the anticipated nucleophilic reaction site.

The true potential of the electrochemical processes in the context of technological applications remains underexplored and underestimated nowadays. We have hereby introduced the idea of CO2 sorption by the negatively charged pristine semiconducting species of carbon, such as graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes, activated carbon, etc. Upon application of voltage, the cathodic carbon atoms gain the necessary nucleophilicities and react with CO2 to give rise to the carboxylated GQD species. The activation barrier of such chemisorption appears surprisingly low. It equals 98 kJ mol−1 for the cathode charge of −1e and 7 kJ mol−1 for the cathode charge of −6e. Whereas, the energetic effect is 92 kJ mol−1 for the −1e charge and −196 kJ mol−1 for the −6e charge. The energetic effects at higher charging degrees appear way more favorable as compared to alkyl amines [56]. The hydration of the scavenging system stabilizes the carboxyl-containing product since it is more polar than the reactants.

Our electronic-structure HDFT simulations for specific fractions of the potential energy surfaces univocally prove the possibility of capturing CO2 at the cathode containing nanoscale or macroscale carbons. Future research must investigate the impact of the graphene sheet size on the energetics of chemisorption. Larger sheets must be simulated to consider the carboxylation of the carbon atoms located farther from the sheet edges. Moreover, it is interesting to consider the carboxylation of the rim carbon atoms since the latter were found to attain the strongest nucleophilicities. By systematically alternating the total charge of the cathode and the number of its atoms, it would be possible to evaluate the balanced parameters of the CO2 scavenger to both obtain high CO2 capacities and preserve the stability of the electrode. Due to the non-uniform distribution of the excessive electron density over the charged carbonaceous cathode, it will be important to search for a minimum reaction path as a function of the target of the captured CO2 molecule.

The provided proof of principle holds promise to open a vibrant avenue of novel solutions to mitigate atmospheric concentrations and valorize CO2. By using straightforward calculations, we linked the voltage and energies associated with the charging of graphene to the macroscopic parameters of the electric current. The evaluated activation barriers and reaction effects urge experimental verification by measuring the direct CO2 capacities of the cathodic nanoscale carbons.

Vitaly V. Chaban: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration.
